description: Foxglove (Digitalis purpurea) is one of the most dangerous plants a cat can encounter. Every part of the plant — fresh or dried — contains potent cardiac glycosides including digoxin, digitoxin, and digitalin. These compounds have an extremely narrow margin between therapeutic and lethal doses, and even small amounts can trigger life-threatening cardiac events in cats.
name: Foxglove
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: cardiac glycosides — digoxin, digitoxin, digitalin, digitonin; all parts toxic including dry leaves, flowers, seeds, and roots
Mechanism: inhibit the Na+/K+-ATPase pump in cardiac muscle → disrupts electrical conduction → arrhythmias and heart block
Symptoms: vomiting, diarrhea, drooling, weakness, bradycardia (abnormally slow heart rate), cardiac arrhythmias, collapse; can be fatal without treatment
Even dried Foxglove in hay or potpourri retains full toxicity
Any suspected ingestion is a veterinary emergency — do not wait for symptoms to develop

symptoms.name: Bradycardia (Slow Heart Rate), Tachycardia (Fast Heart Rate), or Irregular Pulse
symptoms.onset: Within 2 hours (and can persist for days).

symptoms.name: Hyperkalemia (High Potassium Levels)
symptoms.onset: Acute (detectable via blood tests shortly after ingestion).
